behaviors.description: Request with invalid HMAC-SHA256 signature is rejected with 401; body is not forwarded to queue
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the handler either skips signature validation, ignores its result, or forwards the body anyway. A Unit test of the signature validator alone catches validator bugs, but misses: (a) handler not calling the validator, (b) handler calling it but ignoring the result, (c) handler forwarding despite rejection. Only Integration—handler + validator + tracked queue—can verify the entire rejection path: invalid signature → 401 response AND queue.enqueue() never called.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Call handler with valid request body but tampered X-Signature-256 header. Mock the queue with call tracking. Verify: (1) HTTP response is 401, (2) queue.enqueue() was never called, (3) response body explains rejection (e.g., 'Invalid signature'). Vary the signature: correct signature → 200 and enqueue called; corrupted signature → 401 and no enqueue.

behaviors.description: Event is persisted to queue before HTTP 200 response is returned
behaviors.justification: The real failure is async ordering: handler returns 200 before the queue write is durable. Unit tests of enqueue() or response() alone cannot observe ordering. A pure mock queue (in-memory) also hides whether data is truly persisted. Integration—handler + real persistent queue (local DB or filesystem) + instrumented timing—can verify: queue.enqueue() completes and persists before res.send(200) fires. The test must confirm ordering through timing or by reading the queue after response.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Handler + queue with real local storage (SQLite, file system, or in-memory store with durable semantics). Instrument both queue.enqueue() and response sending with timestamps or call order tracking. Send a valid webhook request. After response completes, query the queue to confirm the event was persisted. Verify ordering: event appeared in queue before response was sent. Bonus: use a slow disk or add artificial delay to queue writes to catch race conditions.

behaviors.description: Handler returns HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing to complete
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the handler blocks on downstream processing (e.g., awaits a slow processor), delaying the response. Unit tests with instant mocks hide this. Integration test with a slow-running mock downstream can measure: handler returns before downstream finishes. System tests with real external services add realism but are overkill if the handler's async logic is the concern.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Mock the downstream processor with a function that sleeps for 2 seconds. Call handler with a valid webhook request and measure wall-clock response time. Verify response arrives in <500ms (well before the 2s downstream delay). Repeat with multiple downstream delays (1s, 5s, 10s) to confirm handler is not waiting. Bonus: use Promise.race() or similar to confirm downstream is running in parallel, not sequentially after response.

behaviors.description: Response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even with slow concurrent downstream processing of earlier events
behaviors.justification: The real failure is handler performance degradation under concurrent load with slow downstream. This cannot be observed in isolation (Unit). Unit test of individual request → response is instant with mocks. Integration with concurrent requests and slow mock downstream will reveal: (a) handler accumulates state that blocks later requests, (b) downstream blocking causes cascade slowdown, (c) contention on shared resources. The 3-second SLA is a timing constraint that requires real concurrency measurement.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Fire 10 concurrent HTTP requests to handler, each triggering slow mock downstream (1.5s per event). Record the wall-clock time for each response to arrive. Verify: all 10 responses complete within 3 seconds from their respective request times (not cumulative). If responses are delayed >3s, fail the test. Bonus: vary the downstream delay (0.5s, 1s, 2s) and confirm the handler remains responsive even when total concurrent work exceeds 3s.

behaviors.description: Event with duplicate ID is not written to queue a second time
behaviors.justification: The real failure is handler-level deduplication: either the handler doesn't track received IDs, doesn't check for duplicates, or doesn't prevent re-enqueueing. A Unit test of dedup logic in isolation (e.g., 'does a Set contain this ID?') passes but misses: handler not calling dedup, handler calling dedup but ignoring result, handler enqueueing despite duplicate. Integration—handler + mock queue with call tracking—verifies the full path: duplicate request → dedup check → queue.enqueue() called exactly once.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Mock queue with call counting. Send a webhook request with event ID 'abc123'. Verify queue.enqueue() called once. Send the same request again (same event ID, same body, same signature). Verify queue.enqueue() called exactly once more (total 2 calls, but only 1 for the second request). Repeat with 3+ duplicates. Verify enqueue is called exactly N times, where N is the number of unique event IDs.

behaviors.description: When queue reports it is full, handler returns 503 with Retry-After header
behaviors.justification: The real failure is in the handler's error response logic: either the handler doesn't check for queue-full errors, returns the wrong status code, or omits the Retry-After header. This is pure handler logic (no I/O, no concurrency, no ordering). A Unit test with a mocked queue that returns an error is sufficient: mock queue raises 'QueueFull' error → handler catches → returns 503 with Retry-After. Integration would test the same thing with more overhead.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test: Mock queue to throw or return an error code indicating 'queue full' (e.g., throw new Error('QUEUE_FULL')). Call handler with a valid webhook request. Verify: (1) HTTP response status is 503, (2) Retry-After header is present and contains a sensible value (e.g., '60' seconds), (3) response body is a reasonable error message. Test both queue error codes (if multiple exist): 'QUEUE_FULL' → 503; 'QUEUE_ERROR' → 500 (or handler's default).

behaviors.description: Persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it
behaviors.justification: The real failure is the queue not actually enforcing capacity. A Unit test with in-memory queue and capacity logic (e.g., 'if (queue.size >= capacity) reject') passes but doesn't test persistence: data could vanish on restart. Integration—queue with real local persistence (filesystem, SQLite, etc.)—verifies: (1) queue rejects when full, (2) stored events survive queue restart, (3) capacity is really enforced, not just checked. The word 'persistent' is key: without durable storage, capacity becomes meaningless.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test: Initialize queue with configured capacity (e.g., max 100 events). Use real persistent storage (SQLite database, file-based queue, etc.). Enqueue events until queue reports 'full' error. Verify: enqueue rejects when size ≥ capacity with correct error code. Add one more event after rejection—verify it is rejected. Restart the queue/application; reload the persistent storage. Verify: all previously enqueued events are still there, capacity limit is still enforced. Bonus: confirm that stored events are not lost if the process crashes mid-enqueue.
